title: Credit cards up 11 straight months — longest streak since Jun 2025 purge
body: Credit cards outstanding have grown for 11 consecutive months, extending the streak from 10 periods prior. Streak extended from 10 to 11 periods — the longest run since the 16-period streak that ended in Jun 2025. The current streak began in Jul 2025 and has now surpassed the Oct 2024 level.
implication: The credit card market has established the longest growth run in 11 months — the 11-period streak confirms issuers have re-established expansion momentum after the Oct 2025 purge, and the market is now operating at a structurally higher growth rate than the 2024 baseline. The streak is 5 periods away from matching the 16-period record that ended in Jun 2025, signaling the market is on track to surpass the pre-purge peak within five months if the current pace holds.

chain: The 11-period streak is the longest since Jun 2025, confirming the market has locked into a sustained growth trajectory.
The streak is now in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is operating at a structurally higher growth rate than the 2024 baseline.
The Jun 2025 reset was a one-time event, and the current run confirms the market has entered a new growth phase with no signs of deceleration.

inferences: The 11-period streak is the longest since Jun 2025, confirming the market has locked into a sustained growth trajectory.
The streak is now in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is operating at a structurally higher growth rate than the 2024 baseline.
The Jun 2025 reset was a one-time event, and the current run confirms the market has entered a new growth phase with no signs of deceleration.

title: Credit cards 8.5% YoY — fourth straight month of re-acceleration
body: Credit cards outstanding grew 8.5% year-on-year in May 2026, up 0.3 points from 8.1% prior period. YoY growth re-accelerated for the fourth consecutive period: 8.0% in Mar 2026 → 8.1% in Apr 2026 → 8.5% in May 2026. The rate is now at the upper end of the 17-period range (6.7%–9.4%).
implication: Credit card growth has fully reversed its deceleration trend — the 8.5% rate is the fastest in 13 months, confirming the market has entered a new growth phase after the Oct 2025 purge. The four-period re-acceleration confirms the recovery is complete, and the market is now accelerating toward the Jan 2025 peak (9.4%). The 8.5% rate is in the upper range of the historical distribution, signaling the market is operating at a structurally higher growth rate than the 2024 baseline.

chain: The 8.5% rate is the fastest in 13 months, confirming the purge-driven slowdown has ended and issuers are now expanding at a pace that exceeds the Mar 2025 baseline.
The four-period re-acceleration (7.7% → 8.0% → 8.1% → 8.5%) confirms the market has entered a new growth phase.
The 8.5% rate is only 0.9 points below the Jan 2025 peak (9.4%), signaling the market is on track to return to double-digit growth within two quarters.

inferences: The 8.5% rate is the fastest in 13 months, confirming the purge-driven slowdown has ended and issuers are now expanding at a pace that exceeds the Mar 2025 baseline.
The four-period re-acceleration (7.7% → 8.0% → 8.1% → 8.5%) confirms the market has entered a new growth phase.
The 8.5% rate is only 0.9 points below the Jan 2025 peak (9.4%), signaling the market is on track to return to double-digit growth within two quarters.

title: CC spend YoY — ecommerce 28.6% vs in-store POS 23.1%
body: On a year-on-year basis, credit card ecommerce transaction volume grew 28.6% and in-store POS volume grew 23.1% as of May 2026. Year-on-year removes the heavy seasonality in the monthly numbers — online is outpacing in-store on a clean comparison.
implication: Credit card spend is growing across both channels year-on-year (ecom faster). For credit-risk teams this matters: a spend mix tilting online means more card-not-present volume, where fraud and dispute rates run higher — provisioning and fraud models should track the channel mix, not just the headline spend growth.

chain: CC ecommerce volume up 28.6% YoY vs POS up 23.1% YoY — both growing, seasonally clean
Online (CNP) is the faster-growing channel on a year-on-year basis
Channel mix shift changes the fraud and dispute profile — risk models must track mix, not just total spend

inferences: CC ecommerce volume up 28.6% YoY vs POS up 23.1% YoY — both growing, seasonally clean
Online (CNP) is the faster-growing channel on a year-on-year basis
Channel mix shift changes the fraud and dispute profile — risk models must track mix, not just total spend

title: SFB banks gained CC card share in May 2026 (+0.1pp)
body: SFB banks hold 1.7% of total credit cards outstanding in May 2026 (+0.1pp vs prior month). Private banks lost the most share at -0.1pp, now at 70.9%. SFB growth in credit cards reflects increased fintech partnerships.
implication: SFB banks picking up credit card share — even by 0.1pp — signals a change in who's acquiring customers. SFBs (Small Finance Banks — banks that focus on underserved segments like AU or Equitas) growing in credit cards usually means fintech partnerships or co-branded products are kicking in.For anyone benchmarking credit card portfolio quality, knowing which bank type is gaining share matters — their customer profiles and risk behaviour can be very different.

chain: SFB banks gained 0.1pp CC share — Private banks lost 0.1pp
SFB growth signals fintech partnerships or co-branded card activity in underserved segments
Customer risk profiles differ significantly across bank types — portfolio benchmarking must account for this mix shift

inferences: SFB banks gained 0.1pp CC share — Private banks lost 0.1pp
SFB growth signals fintech partnerships or co-branded card activity in underserved segments
Customer risk profiles differ significantly across bank types — portfolio benchmarking must account for this mix shift

title: Top 5 banks hold 74.1% of CC cards — HDFC BANK LTD leads at 22.1%
body: In May 2026, the top 5 banks account for 74.1% of total credit cards outstanding (-0.1pp vs prior month). HDFC BANK LTD leads with 22.1% share (+0.5% MoM).
implication: 74% of all credit cards in India are with just 5 banks. In practice, this means the national credit card data from RBI tells you largely what HDFC, SBI, ICICI, Axis, and Kotak are doing — not the market as a whole. If your strategy relies on industry-level CC data, keep this concentration in mind.

chain: Top 5 banks hold 74.1% of all CC cards — HDFC BANK LTD alone accounts for 22.1%
RBI aggregate CC data is effectively proxied by these 5 institutions — smaller banks are statistically marginal
Industry-level CC strategy analysis must account for this concentration — it reflects large-bank dynamics, not the full market

inferences: Top 5 banks hold 74.1% of all CC cards — HDFC BANK LTD alone accounts for 22.1%
RBI aggregate CC data is effectively proxied by these 5 institutions — smaller banks are statistically marginal
Industry-level CC strategy analysis must account for this concentration — it reflects large-bank dynamics, not the full market

title: Gap: Foreign bank CC share at 3.6% (-0.0pp MoM) — premium segment shrinking
body: Foreign banks hold only 3.6% of total credit cards outstanding in May 2026, down 0.04pp vs prior month. Foreign banks traditionally serve the high-income, high-spend segment — their declining share signals continued loss of the premium CC market to private Indian banks.
implication: Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered, etc.) traditionally served high-income customers — high credit limits, frequent international travel, premium cards. With their share at just {sh:.1f}% and still falling, those customers are now largely being served by Indian private banks instead. For anyone analysing RBI's credit card data, this means the premium borrower segment is now in the domestic bank numbers — not in a separate foreign bank bucket.

chain: Foreign banks hold only 3.6% of CC cards and declining — premium card segment exiting to Indian private banks
Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered) traditionally served high-income, high-limit, internationally-active customers
Premium cardholder behaviour is under-represented in RBI aggregate CC data — now consolidated into private bank numbers

inferences: Foreign banks hold only 3.6% of CC cards and declining — premium card segment exiting to Indian private banks
Foreign banks (Amex, Standard Chartered) traditionally served high-income, high-limit, internationally-active customers
Premium cardholder behaviour is under-represented in RBI aggregate CC data — now consolidated into private bank numbers

title: ATM volume share 80.61% — stable near median
body: ATM withdrawals represented 80.61% of total debit card transaction volume in May 2026. Share edged down 80.84% → 80.61%, slipping 0.23pp but remaining near the median (79.3%) of the 29-month range.
implication: ATM volume share is stable at 80%–81%, not trending toward 79% as the prior period suggested. The equilibrium is holding — banks should not accelerate digital capacity planning based on a single-period share dip. Monitor for sustained multi-period share loss before revising assumptions.

chain: ATM volume share fell to 80.61% in May 2026, down 0.23pp from 80.84% in Apr 2026.
The share remains near the median (79.3%) and above the 25th percentile (78.1%) — no structural break is evident.
The 0.23pp decline is within normal volatility; the prior period's narrative of a breaking equilibrium is not confirmed.

inferences: ATM volume share fell to 80.61% in May 2026, down 0.23pp from 80.84% in Apr 2026.
The share remains near the median (79.3%) and above the 25th percentile (78.1%) — no structural break is evident.
The 0.23pp decline is within normal volatility; the prior period's narrative of a breaking equilibrium is not confirmed.

title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume — 4 months of decline
body: POS cash withdrawal volume has declined for 4 consecutive months as of May 2026. The decline streak extended from 3 months in Apr 2026 to 4 months in May 2026, following a reset in Jan 2026.
implication: The 4-month decline streak confirms the channel is in terminal collapse with no recovery path. Issuers should accelerate sunset timelines: the feature has no strategic future and is consuming resources for zero value. Complete shutdown immediately.

chain: POS cash withdrawal volume has declined for 4 consecutive months, from 12,351 in Jan 2026 to 7,941 in May 2026.
The 4-month streak confirms the terminal trajectory is intact after the brief Jan 2026 reset.
The channel has no demand floor — each month sets a new all-time low.

inferences: POS cash withdrawal volume has declined for 4 consecutive months, from 12,351 in Jan 2026 to 7,941 in May 2026.
The 4-month streak confirms the terminal trajectory is intact after the brief Jan 2026 reset.
The channel has no demand floor — each month sets a new all-time low.

title: Ecommerce volume share 5.05% — stable at median
body: Ecommerce transactions represented 5.05% of total debit card transaction volume in May 2026. Share edged up 5.02% → 5.05%, gaining 0.03pp and remaining near the median (4.9%–5.7%) of the 29-month range.
implication: Ecommerce volume share is stable at 5.0%, not gaining ground despite ATM visit decline. The beneficiary of ATM share loss is POS, not ecommerce. Banks should expect ecommerce to oscillate in the 4.5%–5.0% range — no breakout is imminent.

chain: Ecommerce volume share rose to 5.05% in May 2026, up 0.03pp from 5.02% in Apr 2026.
The share remains near the median (4.9%–5.7%) and shows no sustained breakout above 5.0%.
The 0.03pp gain is within normal volatility; the 4.5%–5.0% equilibrium is holding.

inferences: Ecommerce volume share rose to 5.05% in May 2026, up 0.03pp from 5.02% in Apr 2026.
The share remains near the median (4.9%–5.7%) and shows no sustained breakout above 5.0%.
The 0.03pp gain is within normal volatility; the 4.5%–5.0% equilibrium is holding.

title: Debit cards 2.8% YoY — flat after Apr 2026 2.7pp deceleration
body: Debit cards outstanding grew 2.8% year-on-year in May 2026, unchanged from 2.8% prior period. YoY growth held at 2.8% for the second consecutive period — unchanged from Apr 2026's 2.8%. The rate is now at the lower end of the 17-period range (2.6%–5.7%).
implication: Debit card growth has stabilised at its slowest pace in 16 months — the 2.8% rate is unchanged from Apr 2026, confirming the sharp deceleration from 5.4% in Mar 2026 has stabilised. The 2.8% rate is only 0.2 points faster than the Jan 2025 trough (2.6%), signaling the market is approaching zero growth. The flat rate confirms the Apr 2026 purge has erased the entire post-recovery acceleration, and the structural growth rate is settling at a lower level than the 2025 baseline.

chain: The 2.8% rate is unchanged from Apr 2026, confirming the sharp deceleration from 5.4% in Mar 2026 to 2.8% in Apr 2026 has stabilised.
The 2.8% rate is only 0.2 points faster than the Jan 2025 trough (2.6%), signaling the market is approaching zero growth.
The flat rate confirms the Apr 2026 purge has erased the entire post-recovery acceleration, and the structural growth rate is settling at a lower level than the 2025 baseline.

inferences: The 2.8% rate is unchanged from Apr 2026, confirming the sharp deceleration from 5.4% in Mar 2026 to 2.8% in Apr 2026 has stabilised.

title: PSB banks hold 64.0% of debit cards — Payments gaining share in May 2026
body: Public sector banks account for 64.0% of total debit cards outstanding in May 2026 (-0.2pp vs prior month). Private banks hold 25.9%. Payments banks are the fastest-growing category (+0.1pp share gain).
implication: PSBs (government-owned banks like SBI, PNB, Bank of Baroda) hold 64.0% of debit cards, largely because of Jan Dhan — the government scheme that opened basic bank accounts for millions of low-income households. Many of these accounts have little activity. If you're using debit transaction data for credit assessment, PSB debit data needs to be treated very differently from, say, HDFC or Kotak debit customers.

chain: PSBs hold 64.0% of debit cards — primarily through Jan Dhan scheme linkage, not active acquisition
Jan Dhan portfolios skew toward low-income, low-activity accounts with thin or no transaction histories
PSB and private bank debit data require separate calibration for transaction-based credit underwriting

inferences: PSBs hold 64.0% of debit cards — primarily through Jan Dhan scheme linkage, not active acquisition
Jan Dhan portfolios skew toward low-income, low-activity accounts with thin or no transaction histories
PSB and private bank debit data require separate calibration for transaction-based credit underwriting

title: STATE BANK OF INDIA leads DC cards at 24.8%
body: STATE BANK OF INDIA holds 24.8% of total debit cards in May 2026 (+0.4% MoM). Top 5 banks account for 50.6% (-0.2pp vs prior month).
implication: Top 5 banks hold 50.6% of debit cards. If you're using debit transaction data for credit underwriting (assessing someone's spending behaviour before giving them a loan), the quality of that data depends heavily on whether these top banks are sharing data with you. Without coverage from at least 2-3 of them, you're missing nearly half the market.

chain: Top 5 banks hold 50.6% of debit cards — STATE BANK OF INDIA alone accounts for 24.8%
Debit transaction data coverage for underwriting depends on data-sharing with these top institutions
Without data from 2-3 of these banks, nearly half the debit market is invisible for credit decisions

inferences: Top 5 banks hold 50.6% of debit cards — STATE BANK OF INDIA alone accounts for 24.8%
Debit transaction data coverage for underwriting depends on data-sharing with these top institutions
Without data from 2-3 of these banks, nearly half the debit market is invisible for credit decisions

title: Gap: DC ATM cash at 80.6% of DC volume — digital transition is incomplete
body: Despite growth in digital payments, ATM cash withdrawals still account for 80.6% of total debit card transaction volume in May 2026 (-0.2pp vs prior month). DC ecommerce is only 5.0% of DC volume. India's debit card base remains overwhelmingly cash-dependent.
implication: 80.6% of debit card spending is ATM cash. Cash leaves no digital record — you can't tell where it was spent or on what. For lenders trying to assess a debit card holder's financial behaviour, the transaction history is mostly blank. Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain the primary tool for this segment — debit transaction data alone isn't enough yet.

chain: 80.6% of DC volume is ATM cash — transactions that leave no digital record
Cash-dominant customers' financial behaviour is opaque — spending categories, frequency, merchants all unknown
Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain essential for this segment; debit transaction data alone is insufficient

inferences: 80.6% of DC volume is ATM cash — transactions that leave no digital record
Cash-dominant customers' financial behaviour is opaque — spending categories, frequency, merchants all unknown
Bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) remain essential for this segment; debit transaction data alone is insufficient

title: Gap: DC ecommerce at 5.0% of DC volume — debit cards leave thin digital footprints
body: Debit card ecommerce transactions account for only 5.0% of total DC transaction volume in May 2026. ATM cash dominates at 80.6%. The vast majority of debit card holders transact primarily via ATM cash withdrawal, with minimal digital payment activity.
implication: Only 5.0% of debit card volume is online spending — the rest is mostly ATM cash. For the typical debit card holder, their transaction history is largely cash withdrawals, which tells you very little about their financial behaviour. To lend to this segment, bureau scores (CIBIL, Experian) and income proxies (salary credits, GST filings) will be far more reliable than transaction data models.

chain: DC ecommerce at only 5.0% of DC volume — debit history is predominantly ATM cash withdrawals
Cash withdrawal records reveal nothing about spending behaviour — categories, merchants, frequency unknown
Income proxies (salary credits, GST filings) and bureau scores are more reliable than transaction models for this segment

inferences: DC ecommerce at only 5.0% of DC volume — debit history is predominantly ATM cash withdrawals
Cash withdrawal records reveal nothing about spending behaviour — categories, merchants, frequency unknown
Income proxies (salary credits, GST filings) and bureau scores are more reliable than transaction models for this segment

title: UPI QR codes 67.7× POS terminals — highest ratio on record
body: There are now 67.7 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in May 2026, up 0.32 from 67.3 in Apr 2026. Ratio rose 67.3× → 67.7×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 0.3 points as UPI QR added 9.0M codes while POS terminals gained only 78K units.
implication: The 67.7× ratio confirms UPI QR is the only acceptance infrastructure with structural expansion momentum — POS terminals are a shrinking asset class with no recovery signal. Lenders financing merchant acceptance should allocate capital exclusively to UPI QR-enabled solutions; the POS channel is contracting while UPI QR scales at 115× the rate. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR adds 7–10M codes monthly and POS struggles to post consecutive gains.

chain: The 67.7× ratio is the maximum level in the 29-period series, up from 37.4× in Jan 2024.
UPI QR added 9.0M codes in May 2026 while POS terminals added 78K units — a 115:1 deployment ratio that confirms the two channels are on opposite trajectories.
The ratio has risen 3.0 points in the past two months (Mar 2026: 64.7× → May 2026: 67.7×), the fastest pace since Nov 2025's 5.8-point surge.

inferences: The 67.7× ratio is the maximum level in the 29-period series, up from 37.4× in Jan 2024.
UPI QR added 9.0M codes in May 2026 while POS terminals added 78K units — a 115:1 deployment ratio that confirms the two channels are on opposite trajectories.
The ratio has risen 3.0 points in the past two months (Mar 2026: 64.7× → May 2026: 67.7×), the fastest pace since Nov 2025's 5.8-point surge.

title: POS terminals -0.5% YoY — first contraction on record
body: POS terminals contracted 0.48% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 1.45% growth in Apr 2026. YoY growth turned negative 1.5% → -0.5%, a 1.9-point drop — the first negative reading in the 17-period series and the minimum level on record.
implication: The -0.5% YoY contraction is a structural break signal — the POS network is now smaller than it was a year ago, the first time this has occurred in the dataset. Lenders financing merchant acceptance infrastructure should treat POS terminals as a declining asset class with no recovery trajectory. The channel has lost 31.5 points of annual growth in 12 months and shows no signs of stabilising.

chain: The -0.5% YoY rate is the first contraction in this dataset, breaking below Apr's 1.5% minimum.
The rate has fallen 32.0 points from May 2025's 31.5% peak, driven by a high base and two months of net declines in Q2 2026.
A negative YoY rate confirms the POS network is shrinking on an annual basis — the channel is in structural decline, not consolidation.

inferences: The -0.5% YoY rate is the first contraction in this dataset, breaking below Apr's 1.5% minimum.
The rate has fallen 32.0 points from May 2025's 31.5% peak, driven by a high base and two months of net declines in Q2 2026.
A negative YoY rate confirms the POS network is shrinking on an annual basis — the channel is in structural decline, not consolidation.

title: UPI QR codes 16.4% YoY — stable high-growth phase
body: UPI QR codes grew 16.43% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.37% in Apr 2026. YoY growth held steady 16.4% → 16.4%, a 0.06-point gain — the rate has stabilised in the 15–17% range across the 17-period series, with May's 16.4% near the median.
implication: The 16.4% YoY rate confirms UPI QR deployment is in a stable, high-growth phase — the network is adding 100M+ codes annually with no signs of saturation. Lenders should treat UPI QR as the primary acceptance infrastructure for digital payments, with a clear structural advantage over POS terminals. The channel is the only one with sustained double-digit growth and a path to 900M codes by mid-2027.

chain: The 16.4% YoY rate has held in the 15–17% range for 13 consecutive months, even as the base has risen from 662M to 780M codes.
The network is adding 110M codes annually at a stable rate, with no signs of saturation or deceleration.
The 16.4% rate is 16.9 points above POS terminals' -0.5% YoY contraction, confirming the two channels are on opposite trajectories.

inferences: The 16.4% YoY rate has held in the 15–17% range for 13 consecutive months, even as the base has risen from 662M to 780M codes.
The network is adding 110M codes annually at a stable rate, with no signs of saturation or deceleration.
The 16.4% rate is 16.9 points above POS terminals' -0.5% YoY contraction, confirming the two channels are on opposite trajectories.

title: UPI QR codes 139× Bharat QR — widest gap on record
body: There are now 139.1 UPI QR codes for every Bharat QR code in May 2026, up 1.87 from 137.3 in Apr 2026. Ratio rose 137.3× → 139.1×, the highest level on record — the gap has widened by 1.9 points, extending the structural divergence that began in Sep 2025.
implication: The 139× ratio confirms Bharat QR has no commercial relevance — UPI QR is the sole acceptance standard for digital payments. Lenders financing merchant infrastructure should allocate zero capital to Bharat QR solutions; the channel is in terminal decline. The ratio will continue widening as UPI QR scales toward 800M codes and Bharat QR remains frozen or shrinks.

chain: The 139.1× ratio is the maximum level in the 29-period series, up from 53.5× in Jan 2024.
The ratio has risen 16.8 points in the past 8 months (Sep 2025: 116.8× → May 2026: 139.1×), accelerating from the 1.0–1.5 point monthly pace seen in H1 2025.
Bharat QR is effectively obsolete — UPI QR is capturing all incremental deployment while Bharat QR stagnates or contracts.

inferences: The 139.1× ratio is the maximum level in the 29-period series, up from 53.5× in Jan 2024.

title: RBL BANK LTD leads POS terminal deployment at 24.0% market share
body: RBL BANK LTD has deployed 2.76M POS terminals in May 2026, accounting for 24.0% of total (+1.3% MoM). Top 5 banks hold 92.8% of all POS terminals (+0.0pp vs prior month).
implication: 5 banks own 92.8% of all POS machines in India. That also means merchant sales data — what shopkeepers sell, how much, how often — sits largely with those same 5 banks. If you want to lend to merchants and need their sales history to decide how much credit to give, you either need a data partnership with one of these banks or an alternate source like GST returns or UPI transaction feeds.

chain: Top 5 banks own 92.8% of POS terminals — merchant acquiring infrastructure is highly concentrated
Merchant transaction data (sales history for credit underwriting) sits with the same institutions
Merchant credit without data partnerships with these banks requires alternate sources — GST returns, UPI transaction feeds

inferences: Top 5 banks own 92.8% of POS terminals — merchant acquiring infrastructure is highly concentrated
Merchant transaction data (sales history for credit underwriting) sits with the same institutions
Merchant credit without data partnerships with these banks requires alternate sources — GST returns, UPI transaction feeds

title: Gap: Top 5 banks hold 92.8% of POS terminals — acquiring market is highly concentrated
body: In May 2026, the top 5 banks account for 92.8% of all deployed POS terminals in India. RBL BANK LTD leads at 24.0% market share. All remaining banks combined share less than 7.2% of merchant acquiring infrastructure.
implication: 92.8% of all POS machines in India are owned by just 5 banks — and so is most of the merchant transaction data that comes with them. If you're building merchant credit products (loans to shopkeepers or small businesses) and don't have data partnerships with these top banks, you're working with an incomplete picture. Alternate sources — GST filings, UPI transaction data — can partially fill this gap.

Merchant credit underwriting without data partnerships with these banks requires alternates — GST filings, UPI feeds
